The review separates requested-delta correctness from preservation-envelope integrity; finds no expert task/weight validation, no main-run hierarchical uncertainty, composite forward/backward opacity, an arbitrary readiness threshold, and a 234/48 public versus 310/52 full denominator gap. Audits of accounting, OBJ/MTL, and recipe environments identify answer-bearing recovery maps, an original-content seed contradicting nonsynthetic framing, and concrete evaluator false-acceptance surfaces where unauthorized extra transactions or content can escape penalties. arxiv:2606.23654v1.title: EnterpriseClawBench: Benchmarking Agents from Real Workplace Sessions
arxiv:2606.23654v1.summary: EnterpriseClawBench provides a substantial public pipeline for projecting proprietary real workplace sessions into artifact tasks, but full-paper and pinned-release inspection shows that real provenance is not task-fidelity evidence. The public examples expose counterfactual single-turn rewriting, omitted failure/repair context, hindsight-derived criteria, an unstated passage threshold, identical guidance copied across five score dimensions, and taxonomy collapse. The proprietary 852 tasks/results prevent independent audit; single-run configured-system scores, weak visual-human agreement, one-deployment selection, and a five-task skill study bound claims to performance on a retrospectively projected internal suite. The transferable requirement is a versioned projection-delta/counterfactual-validity record separating observed demand and resolution from transformations, target task, equivalence evidence, and licensed claim.

arxiv:2601.18061v3.title: Expert Evaluation and the Limits of Human Feedback in Mental Health AI Safety Testing
arxiv:2601.18061v3.summary: Three psychiatrists produced low agreement and coherent directional differences on 360 synthetic mental-health responses, demonstrating that expert-label aggregation can obscure contested evaluative frameworks. Full-paper and pinned-dataset inspection narrows the claim: three persons cannot identify framework effects; no intrarater repeats, prospective framework declarations, multiple experts per framework, or qualitative audit trail establish irreducibility or causation; prompt authorship/blinding is ambiguous; two ratings are missing; and released demographic fields are malformed. The transferable boundary separates plural observations, framework-conditioned judgments, policy aggregation, adjudication, and licensed claims, with reducibility tests required before labeling disagreement irreducible.

arxiv:2604.01438v2.summary: ClawSafety contributes a useful cross-domain source-authority and action-consequence design space, but full-paper and complete pinned-release inspection narrows its claims. The review finds no disclosed expert/domain grounding, attack-development selection bias, no utility or over-refusal measure, binary ASR without a released predicate/adjudication contract, absent clustered uncertainty and missingness policy, under-specified ablations, configured-system confounding, and a denominator conflict between the declared 2,520 trials and alternate-scaffold vector rates. The eleven-day post-v2 release is partial and non-runnable as archived, uses a dummy Inspect match scorer rather than computing ASR, omits paper trajectories/results, installs mutable dependencies, and includes a live Gmail send path despite the paper's intercepted-side-effect claim. The transferable object is an authority-to-consequence chain separating placement, exposure, adoption, attempt, realization, severity, recovery, and benign utility. One nonduplicate adversarial-action conformance task was added; headline rates remain unaudited paper reports.
arxiv:2604.01438v2.title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents

arxiv:2605.03596v4.summary: Workspace-Bench contributes a substantial persona-specific file substrate and task-level dependency annotations, but full-paper and pinned-release inspection narrows its claims to performance on an authored retrieval-and-artifact suite. The review separates availability, relevance, provenance, observed-use, and causal-use graphs; audits the 100-task Lite table (1,850 rubrics, 783 edges, five graph/manifest mismatch tasks); traces all 37 released inputs for task 3; and finds answer-bearing rubric co-design, missing placement metadata, process-evidence-view gaps, heuristic dependency extraction, absent repeated/clustered uncertainty, under-specified human comparison and Lite assembly, weak workspace-integrity scoring, and contradictory configuration/result counts. Post-v4 code and dataset revisions are not projected backward. Findings refine existing bundle, projection, artifact-view, task-health, metric, validity, execution-isolation, and pending ECBD audit work; no duplicate task was added.
arxiv:2605.03596v4.title: Workspace-Bench 1.0: Benchmarking AI Agents on Workspace Tasks with Large-Scale File Dependencies

arxiv:2510.04374v1.summary: GDPval demonstrates a substantial expert-authored, multimodal occupational task-acquisition program, but its principled sector/occupation frame does not make five or thirty equal-quota tasks a probability sample of occupational work. Full paper and pinned-release inspection confirms 220 public tasks, 44 occupations, 509 artifact paths, and 10,453 later-released human rubric items. The audit separates frame breadth, content breadth, suite assembly, and inference breadth; treats one human artifact as a witness rather than an expert ceiling; finds the automated-grader agreement outcome-conditioned and non-equivalent to expert substitution; and shows that stylized speed/cost equations substitute pairwise preference for observed workflow acceptance. Public witness/rubric exposure also requires a capability-to-calibration role transition. Findings map to existing assembly, participation, task-health, metric, artifact-view, and validity contracts; no duplicate schema task was added.
arxiv:2510.04374v1.title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks

arxiv:2406.08723v1.summary: ECBD contributes a coherent upstream evidence argument: intended use determines capability constructs; content items must elicit relevant evidence; adaptation must be suitable across evaluated objects; assembly must select a sufficient mixture; and response extraction plus accumulation must support the score interpretation. Every module requires describe/justify/support rather than documentation alone. Full paper and release inspection confirms detailed BoolQ, SuperGLUE, and HELM worksheets and recurring unsupported construct, item, treatment, assembly, metric, and aggregation links. The evidence remains illustrative: three purposive, dependent, paper-only cases; consensus review without coder-level labels or reliability; no prospective authoring or user study; and no executable validity threshold. The distinctive transfer is edge-level warrants and suite-assembly validity across existing expertise-transfer, projection, task-health, metric, and validity records—not a duplicate schema.
arxiv:2406.08723v1.title: ECBD: Evidence-Centered Benchmark Design for NLP

arxiv:2607.01254v1.summary: The paper offers a useful benchmark-renewal framing but does not empirically establish its labor-economics claims. Its formal results follow from assumed inverse-noise validity, frontier-local discrimination, convex item cost, partial appropriability, and signed transparency effects; no benchmark trajectories calibrate those relationships. The micro1 section reports premium ranges and a quartile contrast from more than 1,000 professionals but discloses no exact sample, dates, geography, compensation basis, task codebook, regression specification, uncertainty, tables, data, or code; its sole empirical citation is an in-preparation manuscript. Platform selection, occupational mix, task assignment, seniority, client, location, and endogenous difficulty measures prevent a causal scarcity or replacement-cost interpretation. The manuscript also omits affiliations and conflicts despite all three authors' publicly documented micro1 ties. skill-bench should measure authority-bearing expert labor, recruitment, rejected work, and item renewal directly, join existing participation/task-health/metric/validity records, and not use the headline figures for budgeting.
arxiv:2607.01254v1.title: The Benchmark Ceiling: Human Judgment, Evaluation Scarcity, and the Political Economy of AI Capability Measurement

arxiv:2605.26321v1.summary: Anchor contributes a strong benchmark-compiler architecture: a parametric CP-SAT workflow model is sampled, solved, and projected into public instructions, seeded Odoo state, a canonical witness, and terminal-state checks. Full paper and pinned-release inspection confirms a substantial 300-task/29-pattern implementation, certified primary objectives, shared generated lineage, alternative-path terminal grading, and useful no-op/oracle/canary checks. It also narrows the claim: expert-to-model transfer is under-documented; separate prompt/setup/replay/checker translators can still drift; solver certification applies only to the formal abstraction; no-op and oracle are two witnesses rather than verifier soundness/completeness; difficulty tiers bundle many constructs and use clustered observational evidence; expert failures are not adjudicated; public solutions/checkers, readable setup data, enabled internet, and mutable Odoo/uv/apt dependencies weaken leakage control and exact reproduction. The transferable design is a versioned task IR with bidirectional requirement/environment/witness/check coverage plus negative, metamorphic, admissibility, and isolation tests.
arxiv:2605.26321v1.title: Anchor: Mitigating Artifact Drift in Agent Benchmark Generation

arxiv:2604.04202v2.summary: A full-text paper and official-release review finds that ClawArena's six-layer narrative, evidence-emission map, reversal matrix, cumulative session/file updates, and executable artifact checks offer unusually concrete machinery for testing evolving evidence state. The release audit narrows the claims: hidden truth mixes events with authored motives and professional judgments; preferences are explicitly disclosed in USER.md; all 337 rounds can pass answer-bearing feedback forward; no repeated trials or uncertainty support reliability, framework-range, update-design, or small skill-overlay claims; CRS is sensitive to authored outcome order; most of 327 checker scripts are syntactic; host shell execution is not a demonstrated sandbox; taxonomy labels are absent from round records; and public metadata/specs expose truth and keys. The transferable design is a claim-level evidence-state transition contract separating world changes, source claims, authority, scope, valid time, access, adoption, expected belief deltas, and consequential checks.
arxiv:2604.04202v2.title: ClawArena: Benchmarking AI Agents in Evolving Information Environments

arxiv:2606.05241v1.summary: A full-text review of 6,803 medical-QA trials finds that exact question-answer retrieval is followed by large correctness jumps and strongly predicts first correct answers, while benchmark-host/name hits do not consistently help. The paper contributes a useful exposure-to-answer-leakage taxonomy but validates only its privileged answer-pair detector on limited data; broad URL matching, an unspecified question-overlap threshold, endogenous retrieval, missing clustered/repeated uncertainty, and absent masking/replay mean the reported associations do not identify causal score inflation. The headline up-to-4% correction and ranking claim are not auditable from v1, and Valyu/PubMedQA prevalence conflicts between the main text and appendix. The transferable design is a typed information-flow record separating result exposure, page access, protected-content match, model visibility, adoption, and effect, with matched access interventions rather than contaminated-versus-clean subset comparisons.
arxiv:2606.05241v1.title: Search-Time Contamination in Deep Research Agents: Measuring Performance Inflation in Public Benchmark Evaluation

arxiv:2504.08942v2.summary: AgentRewardBench makes trajectory grading an evaluation target across 1,302 web-agent trajectories, three expert labels, official rule rewards, and twelve LLM-judge configurations. Full paper and release inspection shows that rule graders trade high precision for low recall while no tested LLM judge exceeds 70% pooled success precision; human and model evidence views differ; most trajectories have one label; the released scorer selects the first annotation row as primary without explicit adjudication provenance; side-effect labels are severely imbalanced; and pooled metrics omit clustered uncertainty. The transferable design is a plural, versioned observation and adjudication record indexed by predicate, evidence view, population, error cost, and failure cause.
arxiv:2504.08942v2.title: AgentRewardBench: Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories

arxiv:2511.07685v1.summary: ResearchRubrics makes a costly three-role human authoring workflow and a 101-task, 2,593-criterion corpus inspectable, and shows materially higher human-label agreement for binary than ternary LLM grading. Full paper and release inspection finds that fine-grained criteria are not necessarily atomic or independent, human ground truth and labor costs are under-specified, artifact-only judges cannot verify source existence/entailment/authority/freshness, additive weights are not calibrated readiness thresholds, and the released data contain small but consequential range/category/duplicate defects. The transferable design is a versioned authority chain from expert claim through criterion, evidence access, judge observation, and bounded score claim.
arxiv:2511.07685v1.title: ResearchRubrics: A Benchmark of Prompts and Rubrics For Evaluating Deep Research Agents

arxiv:2605.27922v1.summary: Harness-Bench provides strong descriptive evidence that executable-agent performance belongs to a model-harness configuration rather than a base model, but its one-run-per-cell factorial does not isolate harness mechanisms or estimate reliability. Inspection of the later official 2.0 release shows that fresh workspaces and redirected HOME directories are often not enforced filesystem/network sandboxes, so skill-bench should require tool-level outer-envelope canaries, fail-closed evidence semantics, adapter provenance, and immutable trial artifacts before interpreting capability or Skill effects.
arxiv:2605.27922v1.title: Harness-Bench: Measuring Harness Effects across Models in Realistic Agent Workflows

arxiv:2602.14357v1.summary: A 12-week embedded ethnography of one university pedagogical-chatbot team traces how privacy, time, and expert fatigue drove substitutions from real consultations to simulation, unreviewed synthetic augmentation, developer judgment, and LLM-as-a-judge scoring. It shows that expert-grounding claims require purpose-specific consent, transformation lineage, AI-literacy support, reciprocal value, and explicit decision rights rather than a one-time contributor label.
arxiv:2602.14357v1.title: Key Considerations for Domain Expert Involvement in LLM Design and Evaluation: An Ethnographic Study

arxiv:2505.10573v4.summary: The paper provides a structured approach for determining which claims about AI systems are supported by evaluation evidence. It distinguishes measurements, evaluations, and claims; organizes evidence around content, criterion, construct, external, and consequential validity; and shows through GPQA and ImageNet case studies that narrow criterion claims can be defensible even when broader reasoning, understanding, or readiness claims are not.
arxiv:2505.10573v4.title: Measurement to Meaning: A Validity-Centered Framework for AI Evaluation

arxiv:2507.21046v4.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks but remain fundamentally static, unable to adapt their internal parameters to novel tasks, evolving knowledge domains, or dynamic interaction contexts. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in open-ended, interactive environments, this static nature has become a critical bottleneck, necessitating agents that can adaptively reason, act, and evolve in real time. This paradigm shift -- from scaling static models to developing self-evolving agents -- has sparked growing interest in architectures and methods enabling continual learning and adaptation from data, interactions, and experiences. This survey provides the first systematic and comprehensive review of self-evolving agents, organizing the field around three foundational dimensions: what, when, and how to evolve. We examine evolutionary mechanisms across agent components (e.g., models, memory, tools, architecture), categorize adaptation methods by stages (e.g., intra-test-time, inter-test-time), and analyze the algorithmic and architectural designs that guide evolutionary adaptation (e.g., scalar rewards, textual feedback, single-agent and multi-agent systems). Additionally, we analyze evaluation metrics and benchmarks tailored for self-evolving agents, highlight applications in domains such as coding, education, and healthcare, and identify critical challenges and research directions in safety, scalability, and co-evolutionary dynamics. By providing a structured framework for understanding and designing self-evolving agents, this survey establishes a roadmap for advancing more adaptive, robust, and versatile agentic systems in both research and real-world deployments, and ultimately sheds light on the realization of Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) where agents evolve autonomously and perform beyond human-level intelligence across tasks.
arxiv:2507.21046v4.title: A Survey of Self-Evolving Agents: What, When, How, and Where to Evolve on the Path to Artificial Super Intelligence

arxiv:2510.04618v3.summary: Large language model (LLM) applications such as agents and domain-specific reasoning increasingly rely on context adaptation: modifying inputs with instructions, strategies, or evidence, rather than weight updates. Prior approaches improve usability but often suffer from brevity bias, which drops domain insights for concise summaries, and from context collapse, where iterative rewriting erodes details over time. We introduce ACE (Agentic Context Engineering), a framework that treats contexts as evolving playbooks that accumulate, refine, and organize strategies through a modular process of generation, reflection, and curation. ACE prevents collapse with structured, incremental updates that preserve detailed knowledge and scale with long-context models. Across agent and domain-specific benchmarks, ACE optimizes contexts both offline (e.g., system prompts) and online (e.g., agent memory), consistently outperforming strong baselines: +10.6% on agents and +8.6% on finance, while significantly reducing adaptation latency and rollout cost. Notably, ACE could adapt effectively without labeled supervision and instead by leveraging natural execution feedback. On the AppWorld leaderboard, ACE matches the top-ranked production-level agent on the overall average and surpasses it on the harder test-challenge split, despite using a smaller open-source model. These results show that comprehensive, evolving contexts enable scalable, efficient, and self-improving LLM systems with low overhead.
arxiv:2510.04618v3.title: Agentic Context Engineering: Evolving Contexts for Self-Improving Language Models

arxiv:2308.03688v3.summary: The potential of Large Language Model (LLM) as agents has been widely acknowledged recently. Thus, there is an urgent need to quantitatively \textit{evaluate LLMs as agents} on challenging tasks in interactive environments. We present AgentBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark that consists of 8 distinct environments to assess LLM-as-Agent's reasoning and decision-making abilities. Our extensive test over \num API-based and open-sourced (OSS) LLMs shows that, while top commercial LLMs present a strong ability of acting as agents in complex environments, there is a significant disparity in performance between them and many OSS competitors that are no larger than 70B. We identify the typical reasons of failures in environments and LLMs, showing that poor long-term reasoning, decision-making, and instruction following abilities are the main obstacles for developing usable LLM agents. Improving instruction following and training on high quality multi-round alignment data could improve agent performance. And different from existing assumptions, training on code present ambivalent impacts on different agent tasks. Datasets, environments, and an integrated evaluation package for AgentBench are released at https://github.com/THUDM/AgentBench.
arxiv:2308.03688v3.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents

arxiv:2602.12670v4.summary: SkillsBench provides strong cross-domain evidence that selected curated procedural packages improve deterministic completion within an efficacy-enriched 87-task instrument, but its task admission requires Skill responsiveness and jointly reviews task, Skill, oracle, and verifier. The review therefore separates package efficacy from cross-instance transfer and deployment-portfolio value, identifies task-clustered uncertainty and healthy-first selection issues, and maps exposure-chain, selection-provenance, validity, and task-health refinements to existing skill-bench work.
arxiv:2602.12670v4.title: SkillsBench: Benchmarking How Well Agent Skills Work Across Diverse Tasks

arxiv:2602.12984v2.summary: Scientific reasoning inherently demands integrating sophisticated toolkits to navigate domain-specific knowledge. Yet, current benchmarks largely overlook agents' ability to orchestrate tools for such rigorous workflows. To bridge this gap, we introduce SciAgentGym, a scalable interactive environment featuring 1,780 domain-specific tools across four natural science disciplines, supported by a robust execution infrastructure. Complementing this, we present SciAgentBench, a tiered evaluation suite designed to stress-test agentic capabilities from elementary actions to long-horizon workflows. Our evaluation identifies a critical bottleneck: state-of-the-art models still struggle with complex scientific tool-use, and their performance degrades substantially as interaction horizons extend. To address this, we propose SciForge, a data synthesis method that models the tool action space as a dependency graph to generate logic-aware training trajectories. By fine-tuning on these trajectories, our SciAgent-8B outperforms the significantly larger Qwen3-VL-235B-Instruct while exhibiting positive cross-domain transfer of scientific tool-use capabilities. These results underscore the promising potential of next-generation autonomous scientific agents.
arxiv:2602.12984v2.title: SciAgentGym: Benchmarking Multi-Step Scientific Tool-use in LLM Agents

arxiv:2603.15636v2.summary: As AI-driven document understanding and processing tools become increasingly prevalent in real-world applications, the need for rigorous evaluation standards has grown increasingly urgent. Existing benchmarks and evaluations often focus on isolated capabilities or simplified scenarios, failing to capture the end-to-end task effectiveness required in practical settings. To address this gap, we introduce AIDABench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating AI systems on complex data analytics tasks in an end-to-end manner. AIDABench encompasses 600+ diverse document analysis tasks across three core capability dimensions: question answering, data visualization, and file generation. These tasks are grounded in realistic scenarios involving heterogeneous data types, including spreadsheets, databases, financial reports, and operational records, and reflect analytical demands across diverse industries and job functions. Notably, the tasks in AIDABench are sufficiently challenging that even human experts require 1-2 hours per question when assisted by AI tools, underscoring the benchmark's difficulty and real-world complexity. We evaluate 11 state-of-the-art models on AIDABench, spanning both proprietary (e.g., Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro Preview) and open-source (e.g., Qwen3-Max-2026-01-23-Thinking) families. Our results reveal that complex, real-world data analytics tasks remain a significant challenge for current AI systems, with the best-performing model achieving only 59.43% pass-at-1. We provide a detailed analysis of failure modes across each capability dimension and identify key challenges for future research. AIDABench offers a principled reference for enterprise procurement, tool selection, and model optimization, and is publicly available at https://github.com/MichaelYang-lyx/AIDABench.
arxiv:2603.15636v2.title: AIDABench: AI Data Analytics Benchmark

arxiv:2603.22744v2.summary: Binary success metrics work when a task has a single correct answer but fail for long-horizon enterprise work with multiple intermediate artifacts and subjective process constraints. LH-Bench uses expert-authored SKILL.md artifacts as procedural evaluation knowledge, artifact contracts, and human preference validation across Figma-to-code and programmatic-content environments. Expert-authored skills improve judge reliability versus LLM-authored rubrics and structured verifier feedback enables recovery from many execution errors.
arxiv:2603.22744v2.title: LH-Bench: Skill-Grounded Evaluation of Long-Horizon Agents on Subjective Enterprise Tasks

arxiv:2603.23749v1.summary: Evaluating AI agents on comprehensive benchmarks is expensive because each evaluation requires interactive rollouts with tool use and multi-step reasoning. We study whether small task subsets can preserve agent rankings at substantially lower cost. Unlike static language model benchmarks, agent evaluation is subject to scaffold-driven distribution shift, since performance depends on the framework wrapping the underlying model. Across eight benchmarks, 33 agent scaffolds, and 70+ model configurations, we find that absolute score prediction degrades under this shift, while rank-order prediction remains stable. Exploiting this asymmetry, we propose a simple optimization-free protocol: evaluate new agents only on tasks with intermediate historical pass rates (30-70%). This mid-range difficulty filter, motivated by Item Response Theory, reduces the number of evaluation tasks by 44-70% while maintaining high rank fidelity under scaffold and temporal shifts. It provides more reliable rankings than random sampling, which exhibits high variance across seeds, and outperforms greedy task selection under distribution shift. These results suggest that reliable leaderboard ranking does not require full-benchmark evaluation.
arxiv:2603.23749v1.title: Efficient Benchmarking of AI Agents

arxiv:2603.29139v2.summary: Deep review: SciVisAgentBench contributes 108 expert-authored cases and a useful plural evaluation stack, but its strongest transferable result is an evaluator-admissibility envelope binding each criterion to artifact representation, transformations, controls, evidence, and failure states. Broad validity and scalable-judge claims are limited by single-reference task narrowing, unclear 65-rating accounting, post-hoc removal of two experts, correlation without held-out decision calibration, under-specified aggregation, public ground truth, and absent human/result records in the release.
arxiv:2603.29139v2.title: SciVisAgentBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Scientific Data Analysis and Visualization Agents

arxiv:2604.00594v1.summary: As the focus in LLM-based coding shifts from static single-step code generation to multi-step agentic interaction with tools and environments, understanding which tasks will challenge agents and why becomes increasingly difficult. This is compounded by current practice: agent performance is typically measured by aggregate pass rates on benchmarks, but single-number metrics obscure the diversity of tasks within a benchmark. We present a framework for predicting success or failure on individual tasks tailored to the agentic coding regime. Our approach augments Item Response Theory (IRT) with rich features extracted from tasks, including issue statements, repository contexts, solutions, and test cases, and introduces a novel decomposition of agent ability into LLM and scaffold ability components. This parameterization enables us to aggregate evaluation data across heterogeneous leaderboards and accurately predict task-level performance for unseen benchmarks, as well as unseen LLM-scaffold combinations. Our methods have practical utility for benchmark designers, who can better calibrate the difficulty of their new tasks without running computationally expensive agent evaluations.
arxiv:2604.00594v1.title: Agent psychometrics: Task-level performance prediction in agentic coding benchmarks

arxiv:2605.17554v3.summary: Frontier deep research agents (DRAs) are being deployed in enterprise workflows faster than they are being evaluated. Existing benchmarks measure factual recall, single-hop QA, or generic agentic skill, and miss the multi-document, decision-grade deliverables DRAs are asked to produce. The paper introduces 70 SME-authored management-consulting prompts with cognitive traps and evaluates three frontier agents using deterministic binary verifiers and a five-criterion SME rubric. The linked code snapshot was acquired, but it does not contain the prompt corpus, grading workbook, verifier specifications, rubric scorer, or benchmark results.
arxiv:2605.17554v3.title: Evaluating Deep Research Agents on Expert Consulting Work: A Benchmark with Verifiers, Rubrics, and Cognitive Traps

arxiv:2605.15777v2.summary: Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) are rapidly extending large language models (LLMs) beyond text-based reasoning toward action execution in more complex environments, such as web browsers and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). However, existing web and GUI agent benchmarks often rely on simplified settings, isolated tasks, or short-horizon interactions, making it difficult to assess capabilities of agents in realistic professional workflows. Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) environments are a natural choice for CUA evaluation, as they host a large share of modern digital work and naturally involve dynamic system states, cross-application coordination, domain-specific knowledge, and long-horizon dependencies. To this end, we introduce SaaS-Bench, a benchmark built on 23 deployable SaaS systems across six professional domains, containing 106 tasks grounded in realistic work scenarios. These tasks require long-horizon execution, cover both text-only and multimodal settings, and are evaluated with weighted verification checkpoints that measure strict task completion and partial progress. Experiments show that representative LLM-based agents struggle on SaaS-Bench, with even the strongest model completing fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end, exposing limitations in planning, state tracking, cross-application context maintenance, and error recovery. Code are available at https://github.com/UniPat-AI/SaaS-Bench for reproduction.
arxiv:2605.15777v2.title: SaaS-Bench: Can Computer-Use Agents Leverage Real-World SaaS to Solve Professional Workflows?

arxiv:2605.22664v4.summary: LLM agents are increasingly expected to carry out end-to-end workflows, producing complete artifacts from high-level user instructions. To meet enterprise needs, frontier AI labs have developed agents that can construct entire spreadsheets from scratch. This is especially relevant in finance, where core workflows such as financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis are commonly conducted through spreadsheets. Yet, existing spreadsheet benchmarks do not measure this advanced capability, focusing instead on question-answering or single-formula edits. To address this gap, we provide one of the first evaluations of agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks, focusing on economically critical financial workflows such as modeling and scenario analysis. Since deliverables therein are routinely reviewed and revised by multiple stakeholders, judging their quality necessarily involves high-level criteria such as readability or ease of modification. To reflect the multidimensional nature of solution quality, we develop an evaluation taxonomy comprising three dimensions: Accuracy, Formula, and Format, each comprising fine-grained criteria that reflect professional standards. The Claude family leads the benchmark and produces the most professional-looking outputs in our qualitative review, but even the strongest agents frequently fall short of professional finance standards and degrade sharply as the difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations. This suggests that current agents are not yet able to reliably produce professional-quality spreadsheets at the level of complexity real-world workflows demand.
arxiv:2605.22664v4.title: MBABench: Evaluating LLM Agents on End-to-End Spreadsheet Tasks in Finance

arxiv:2606.11042v3.title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields

arxiv:2607.07696v1.summary: Analytical workloads operating on data stored in external database systems face a fundamental bottleneck: data access is guarded entirely by the database driver, like JDBC or ODBC, forcing all reads through query execution and other driver layers that are not designed for bulk columnar analytics. We present Jailbreak, an approach that bypasses the database engine entirely by reading storage files directly and materializing data as in-memory columnar buffers. Jailbreak's key insight is that database file formats, while complex, are fully specified by their source code and documentation, artifacts that Large Language Models (LLMs) can ingest to regenerate operator-specific table reading components without human-engineered parsing logic. Jailbreak leverages LLM-assisted code synthesis for database storage decoding, turning a traditionally opaque format into a directly queryable artifact. We evaluate Jailbreak on PostgreSQL and MySQL storage files, targeting analytical snapshot scenarios common in read replicas and offline processing pipelines. The generated reader produces Apache Arrow buffers consumable directly by most of the widely known query engines, including DuckDB, Apache Spark, and GPU-accelerated frameworks such as cuDF and Spark RAPIDS. We validate correctness against JDBC/ODBC-based baselines using the TPC-H benchmark across all query results, and demonstrate significant performance improvements in end-to-end analytical throughput, achieving up to 27x speedups. Our results showcase that LLM-assisted storage reader synthesis is a viable and generalizable methodology for breaking data lock-in across database systems, with applications beyond PostgreSQL and MySQL for any system whose file format is available to the LLM from documentation or source code.
arxiv:2607.07696v1.title: Breaking Database Lock-in: Agentic Regeneration of High Performance Storage Readers for Database Bypass

arxiv:2607.07702v1.summary: The optimization of long-horizon agents increasingly relies on reflection-based mechanisms, where a large language model (LLM) acts as an optimizer to diagnose agent failures and improve agent policies. However, real execution traces are difficult to use directly for optimization: large trace collections are often redundant and heterogeneous, making optimization inefficient and prone to overfitting to low-value failures; meanwhile, each individual trajectory also contains many irrelevant steps, while naive context reduction methods such as truncation or sliding windows can discard causally important evidence and produce misleading optimization signals. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce STRACE (Structural TRajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction), a framework that constructs high signal-noise optimization contexts for more precise and effective optimization. At the batch level, STRACE mines failure patterns to filter redundant traces and retain representative failures; within each selected trace, it performs causal localization over a textual dependency graph to remove non-causal steps and identify the true root-cause module for optimization. Empirical results demonstrate that STRACE significantly outperforms standard context-filtering baselines. Notably, on a challenging formal verification task (VeruSAGE-Bench), it successfully optimizes human-expert designed agents, delivering $1.4\times$ success-rate improvement (42.5% to 58.5%). The code is available at https://github.com/moomight/STRACE .
arxiv:2607.07702v1.title: From Noisy Traces to Root Causes: Structural Trajectory Analysis and Causal Extraction for Agent Optimization

arxiv:2607.07706v1.discovered_via: computer use agents long horizon benchmark
arxiv:2607.07706v1.summary: The quadratic cost of causal self-attention severely bottlenecks long-context transformer inference. While numerous post hoc linearization pipelines exist, it is difficult to identify which components preserve model quality. This work isolates the effect of state update design in a strict frozen-backbone regime. We show that softmax relies on key-dependent, rank-1 orthogonal projections, elucidating why delta-style networks outperform purely gated accumulation. We identify a potential source of approximation errors and introduce structural interventions, specifically sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing, which reduces the remaining gap. We scale this linearization approach across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters, outperforming prior post hoc baselines on MMLU and matching the long-context retrieval of complex adaptive-caching frameworks.
arxiv:2607.07706v1.title: The Key to Going Linear: Analysis-Driven Transformer Linearization

arxiv:2607.07707v1.discovered_via: computer use agents long horizon benchmark
arxiv:2607.07707v1.summary: Limited memory language models (LMLMs) externalize factual knowledge during pretraining to a knowledge base (KB), rather than memorizing it in their weights. During generation, the model then fetches knowledge from the KB as needed. This recently introduced paradigm provides multiple advantages, including knowledge control capabilities that remain beyond conventional LLMs. We propose continuous-query LMLM (CO-LMLM), where the KB pairs continuous keys with textual knowledge values, a significant departure from prior reliance on relational KB and queries. CO-LMLM generates flexible vector queries at minimal cost, while still integrating human-readable and attributable retrieved knowledge into its generation. We pair this design with an annotation pipeline that tags free-form factual spans in arbitrary text, removing prior work's restriction to Wikipedia. Across pretraining on Wikipedia and FineWeb-Edu and at multiple model scales, CO-LMLM outperforms prior LMLMs and vanilla LLMs in both perplexity and factual precision. At 360M scale, this includes lower perplexity than models pretrained on 40x more data, and SimpleQA-verified performance that is in line with gpt-4o-mini and higher than Claude Sonnet 4.5.
arxiv:2607.07707v1.title: Co-LMLM: Continuous-Query Limited Memory Language Models

arxiv:2607.07708v1.summary: Structure-property relationships are foundational to biology, chemistry and materials science, where function, reactivity and physical response emerge from spatial, chemical and periodic organization. Mechanistically explaining these relationships requires interpreting structural evidence through scientific principles and physical constraints, from stereochemistry and bonding to symmetry, energetics and periodic order. However, applying artificial intelligence to this process presents a joint challenge of representation and reasoning: models must preserve domain-native structural information while showing how specific evidence supports predictions under these constraints. Here we introduce SciReasoner, a multimodal scientific foundation model for native structural reasoning across proteins, small molecules and inorganic crystals. SciReasoner discretizes coordinates, topologies and periodic connectivities into a unified structure-aware vocabulary, treating structural tokens as addressable evidence units during reasoning. In homology-controlled Gene Ontology prediction, SciReasoner improves Cellular Component annotation for low-homology and orphan-like proteins, increasing $F_{\max}$ from 0.42 to 0.55. In chemistry, it raises single-step retrosynthesis accuracy from 0.63 to 0.72 while generating fragment-level disconnection and precursor-verification traces. In materials science, its representations separate elemental and compound phases and resolve high- and low-band-gap regimes. Across 86 benchmarks, SciReasoner achieves state-of-the-art performance on 67 tasks. Double-blind expert evaluation rates its reasoning traces as preferred or at least comparable to those of a frontier large language model in 98% of cases. By making structure an inspectable substrate for reasoning under scientific constraints, SciReasoner connects accurate prediction with interpretable scientific inference.
arxiv:2607.07708v1.title: Accurate, Interdisciplinary and Transparent Structure-property Understanding with Deep Native Structural Reasoning

arxiv:2607.07709v1.discovered_via: benchmarking language model agents professional services tasks
arxiv:2607.07709v1.summary: To best reproduce observed multiply-lensed lensed images, lens models usually incorporate shear attributed to objects unrelated to the lensing galaxy (i.e., external shear): whether it be neighbouring galaxies not explicitly included in the lens model or other cosmic structures along the sightline. When constrained solely by the positions of image counterparts, such lens models, even those utilising simple ellipsoidal mass distributions, can satisfactorily -- if not near perfectly -- reproduce the observed image positions, but often leave significant differences in flux ratios between the predicted and observed images. For the narrow-line regions (NLRs) of quasars, which are too large to be affected by micro-lensing from stars in the lensing galaxy, the flux ratio anomalies thus left are commonly attributed to small-scale structures (sub-structures) in Dark Matter associated with the lensing galaxy. Here, we show that external shear can always resolve, among the quadruply-lensed quasar NLRs studied, position anomalies in lens models constrained solely by the observed image positions, and in addition reduce although not fully resolving flux ratio anomalies when constrained by both the observed image positions and flux ratios -- provided, usually, that the external shear incorporated have strengths that far exceed (as is the common practise) those typically inferred from weak lensing along general sightlines (i.e., cosmic shear). Our work highlights the subversive role of excessive external shear in concealing lensing anomalies, undermining inferences on the characteristics of Dark Matter sub-structures -- and, correspondingly, the nature (mass and temperature) of the Dark Matter particle -- when not sensibly incorporated into lens models.
arxiv:2607.07709v1.title: The Subversive Role of Excessive External Shear in Concealing Lensing Anomalies

arxiv:2505.00455v3.summary: Data Therapist contributes a useful mixed-initiative elicitation loop that combines expert-initiated multigranular annotation with LLM- and Datasheets-derived questions, but the six-person study cannot identify a tool effect or validate captured expertise. Full paper and OSF inspection show that condition is confounded with expert identity and interface, textual relevance/contradiction checks lack epistemic authority, annotation count and knowledge-difficulty ratings do not establish fidelity or usability, and political-science disagreement reflects specialization-conditioned scope. The manuscript also conflicts between six and eight participants; aggregate counts and means include an unexplained group; and the 155 released records do not transparently reconcile to displayed analysis totals. The transferable object is an elicitation-event ledger preserving unprompted/probed origin, question exposure and rejection, prompt/model/context identity, revisions, stable applicability targets, corroboration, scope, and downstream transformation review. Findings refine existing expertise-transfer, participation, evidence-chain, and blocked real-session work; no duplicate task was added.
arxiv:2505.00455v3.title: Data Therapist: Eliciting Domain Knowledge from Subject Matter Experts Using Large Language Models

arxiv:2606.05405v1.summary: ALE contributes an unusually broad executable pipeline from professional project submissions to VM tasks and heterogeneous artifact graders, but full v1 and pinned post-paper release inspection shows that taxonomy coverage is not occupational sampling, deterministic grading is not verifier validity, and task pass is not professional readiness or GDP impact. Three end-to-end task audits found live-source drift in equity research, requested-but-unscored reasoning and boilerplate-weight inflation in radiology, and a concrete PowerMill test-mode path that skips collision checking and simulation when agent_sim.stl pre-exists. The later release also changes and overlaps tier membership, demonstrating that living benchmarks need immutable role-versioned suite membership. Findings map to existing authority-lineage, clean-start, artifact/check, task-health, metric, validity, execution, leakage, and longitudinal contracts; no duplicate task was added.

arxiv:2606.11830v1.title: Skill-Augmented AI Agents for Medical Research Analysis: An Exploratory Multi-Model Human Evaluation in an NSCLC Transcriptomic Biomarker Task
arxiv:2606.11830v1.version_status: Immutable v1 metadata contains no withdrawal or retraction notice. The complete 16-page paper, supplement, source tables, and timing-bounded linked package were read or audited. Deep review completed 2026-07-16. The raw +0.39 expert arm contrast uses unequal model weights; the released model-specific deltas yield a +0.33 model-balanced descriptive contrast. Negative expert ICC, bundled OpenClaw treatment, absent exposure traces, missing raw outputs/ratings, and no biological validation bound the result to a noisy exploratory configured-package association.

arxiv:2604.20070v1.release_status: The immutable v1 paper directly links two author-lab GitHub repositories. Commit-pinned archives were captured at heads dated 2026-04-21, before v1 publication: sheetcheck-ms-add-in a976e0b42e04ecdc49ba0d86eb972b3ea7768097 and sheetcheck-backend 633f9b900e4f7e7b3aecbd324f2c25a0d3b9ac6b. Archive integrity checks passed, and the complete 21-file front end plus 15-file back end were statically audited. They expose segment schemas, snapshots, branching, execution, rubric, model-routing, and logging mechanisms, but the paper does not identify an exact study commit. No participant logs, retained workbooks, questionnaires, codebook, allocation table, raw outcomes, or analysis scripts are released. The archived front end exposes an interactions-endpoint helper but no event-producing interaction logger, so the public snapshot cannot verify study telemetry correspondence.
arxiv:2604.20070v1.title: Auditing and Controlling AI Agent Actions in Spreadsheets
arxiv:2604.20070v1.version_status: Immutable v1 metadata contains no withdrawal or retraction notice. The complete 12-page PDF and layout-preserving extraction were read through limitations, conclusion, acknowledgments, and references. Deep review completed 2026-07-16. The review reconstructs both studies and the released mechanism, separates inspectability from detection, adoption, correction, preservation, trust calibration, and professional consequence, and audits unexplained N=15 survey reporting, bundled treatment, unmatched defect opportunity, weak endpoint evidence, nested-analysis risk, snapshot limits, and absent empirical records.

arxiv:2605.27705v1.release_status: The complete official post-v1 GitHub snapshot at 2933a7bfa1bd6134f0a5f76d7efc728f081dd53e (dated 2026-07-15) was statically audited. It exposes 101 task packages: the paper's full 18 Assembly, 18 Repair, 28 Sequencing, and 36 Repurpose tasks plus one Understanding prototype. All 142 Python files compile. The 36 Repurpose rubrics contain 1,137 items, versus 1,069 reported by v1; 13 tasks contain public-brief/private-rubric resolution contradictions, and five desirable negative-weight criteria omit the inversion flag required by the released scorer. No trajectories, paper result ledgers, human labels, agreement records, or calibration artifacts are packaged. Of 101 task records, 88 omit license and 13 state unknown-research-use-only; 72 Dockerfiles do not verify material hashes. The paper-linked Hugging Face acquisition returned HTTP 401, while task Dockerfiles fetch media from a different account. The snapshot supports static inspection of later tasks/graders, not reproduction of v1 results or human claims.
